summary: Gruber uses an elaborate satirical framing device — pretending to take calls from an Apple 'payola rep' — to dismantle Dan Lyons's Newsweek piece claiming Apple is in crisis over the iPhone 4 antenna issue. He methodically quotes Lyons's assertions and then undercuts each one by pointing to the actual evidence: Jobs did acknowledge the antenna issue at the press conference, other phones demonstrably have similar problems, and the vast majority of iPhone 4 owners are satisfied. The sustained irony works by pretending to agree with Lyons while making his claims look absurd against the facts. Gruber's core argument is that Lyons's piece is built on a false premise — that Apple denied there was any problem — when the press conference transcript shows otherwise.
key_points: Lyons claimed Apple refused to acknowledge the antenna problem, but Gruber quotes Jobs explicitly acknowledging the grip issue and offering free cases to address it
The satirical 'Apple payola' framing mocks the common accusation that Gruber is an Apple shill, while simultaneously making the strongest possible case for Apple's position
Lyons's claim that Apple 'invented video chat' with FaceTime is presented as emblematic of his tendency to exaggerate and mischaracterize
Apple's evidence showing other phones dropping bars — and independent YouTube videos confirming it — contradicts Lyons's assertion that the problem is unique to iPhone 4
The piece argues that Lyons's narrative of Apple in crisis is disconnected from the reality of overwhelming customer satisfaction and strong sales
money_quotes: So I get another call from my payola rep at Apple, and she's like, 'Hey, thanks so much for all the antenna-related links over the weekend.'
Which is a polite way to start, because, well, we all know just how panicked everyone at Apple is feeling these days about the company's prospects.
I went back through my notes on the press conference, looking for the part where Jobs said there was no problem.
But where's the acknowledgement that the iPhone 4 antenna is this year's Ishtar? Where's the product recall? Where's the 'KICK ME' sign on Steve Jobs's black shirt?
That's the uncomfortable truth. The last honest man is Dan Lyons.
Lyons doesn't name all those phones on which people are video chatting every day, because he doesn't need to. Just look around and see them for yourself.
People are going to look back at this piece a year from now and say, 'By god, Dan Lyons saw it all along.' (Seriously: bookmark it.)

It's bad enough that I don't have the courage to call Apple out on this blatant chicanery; the last thing I'm going to do is put my name on the line and argue that he's a big dummy with a chip on his shoulder.
key_insight: By pretending to refuse to criticize Dan Lyons while systematically demolishing every one of his claims, Gruber exposes how Antennagate coverage was built on mischaracterization of what Apple actually said and did.
